div.p: Gods; and then we shall see as we are seen,1 and be as our God2 and He as His Father.3 I want to reason a little on this subject. I learned it by translating the papyrus which is now in my house.

I learned a testimony concerning Abraham, and he reasoned concerning the God of heaven.4 “In order to do that,” said he, “suppose we have two facts:5 that supposes another fact may exist—two men on the earth, one wiser than the other, would logically show that another who is wiser than the wisest may exist. Intelligences exist one above another,6 so that there is no end to them.”

If Abraham reasoned thus—If Jesus Christ was the Son of God, and John discovered that God the Father of Jesus Christ7 had a Father,8 you may suppose that He had a Father also. Where was there ever a son without a father? And where was there ever a father without first being a son? Whenever did a tree or anything spring into existence without a progenitor? And everything comes in this way. Paul says that which is earthly is in the likeness of that which is heavenly,9 Hence if Jesus had a Father,10 can we not believe that He had a Father also? I despise the idea of being scared to death at such a doctrine, for the Bible is full of it.

I want you to pay particular attention to what I am saying. Jesus said that the Father wrought precisely in the same way as His Father had done before Him.11 As the Father had done before? He laid down His life, and took it up12 the same as His Father had done before. He did as He was sent, to lay down His life and take it up again; and then was committed unto Him the keys.13 I know it is good reasoning.

I have reason to think that the Church is being purged.14 I saw Satan fall from heaven,15 and the way they ran was a caution. All these are wonders16 and marvels in our eyes17 in these last days. So long as men are under the law of God,18 they have no fears—they do not scare themselves.
I want to stick to my text, to show that when men open their lips against these truths19 they do not injure me, but injure themselves. To the law and to the testimony,20 for these prin-
